1: 'If a prophet or a dreamer of dreams arises among you' — chapter 13 describes three scenarios of enticement to apostasy: (1) a prophet or dreamer who performs signs (Deuteronomy 13:1-5); (2) a close family member who privately entices (Deuteronomy 13:6-11); (3) a city that collectively apostatizes (Deuteronomy 13:12-18). The three cases escalate in social scope (individual → family → community) and in the severity of response required. The prophet/dreamer scenario addresses the most credible form of threat: a figure with apparent divine endorsement.
2: 'And that sign or wonder actually comes to pass, while he says, “Let us go after other gods”' — the disturbing scenario: a prophet whose predictions or miracles are confirmed but who directs the people to other gods. In Deuteronomy's theology, predictive success alone does not validate a prophet; the critical test is the content of the message. A functionally successful prophet who redirects Israel away from YHWH is definitively a false prophet regardless of the sign's occurrence. The sign authenticates prophetic capacity, not prophetic message.
3: 'The LORD your God is testing you to find out whether you love the LORD your God with all your heart and with all your soul' — the trial theology: false prophets with apparent signs are part of YHWH's testing of covenant loyalty. The same testing language is used for Abraham (Genesis 22:1) and Israel in the wilderness (Exodus 16:4; Deuteronomy 8:2). The test reveals whether Israel's covenant loyalty is robust enough to withstand credible supernatural challenge — whether love for YHWH is prior to and independent of any sign or wonder.
4: 'You shall follow the LORD your God, fear him, keep his commandments, obey his voice, serve him, and hold fast to him' — the six-part covenant-loyalty formula is the positive response to the false prophet's temptation. Each element describes a different dimension of the covenant relationship: following (alignment of direction), fearing (reverent awe), keeping (observance), obeying (responsive action), serving (cultic and moral practice), holding fast (relational adherence). The comprehensiveness of the formula underlines that covenant loyalty cannot be partial.
5: 'That prophet or dreamer must be put to death, because he has incited rebellion against the LORD your God' — the death penalty for false prophecy that leads to apostasy. The reasoning is juridical: the prophet has committed treason against the divine suzerain by inciting rebellion (dibbēr-sārāh: spoken apostasy/rebellion). In ANE treaty law, anyone who induced the vassal to break covenant with the suzerain was guilty of capital treason. 'Purge the evil from your midst' (ûbiʿartā hārāʿ miqqirbĕkā) is Deuteronomy's standard purging formula — the community bears responsibility for maintaining covenant integrity.
6: 'If your brother — your mother's son — or your son or daughter, or the wife of your embrace, or your closest friend secretly entices you' — the second scenario targets the most intimate social bonds. The specific kinship terms escalate in relational intimacy: full sibling (mother's son), child, spouse (wife of your embrace = the most intimate relationship of the household), and best friend. The phrase 'secretly entices' (yĕsîtĕkā bassēter) describes private, whispered solicitation outside the public covenant assembly — a more insidious threat than the public prophet because it operates in the space of trust.
7: 'Gods of the peoples around you, whether near or far, from one end of the earth to the other' — the geographical scope of the temptation: not merely the local Canaanite cults but any foreign deity from any direction. The comprehensive formulation reflects Israel's position at the crossroads of ANE civilization — Egyptian deities to the south, Mesopotamian to the east, Phoenician to the north, and Philistine to the west. The temptation to supplement YHWH worship with the religious practices of neighboring cultures was a constant reality, not a hypothetical danger.
8: 'You shall not consent to him, nor listen to him. Your eye shall not pity him; you shall not spare him or conceal him' — four prohibitions governing the response to a family member's apostasy-enticement. The explicit 'do not pity' and 'do not spare' override the powerful family-loyalty instincts that honor-shame culture demanded. In a society where family honor required protecting and advocating for kin at all costs, the command to refuse protection to an apostatizing family member was shockingly counter-cultural.
9: 'Rather, you shall certainly put him to death. Your hand shall be the first raised against him, and afterward the hand of all the people' — the first-hand requirement for the one who was enticed is psychologically significant: the person who received the private solicitation must be the first to act against the solicitor. This prevents the accused from claiming that only their enemies convicted them — the victim-turned-accuser's first action establishes both their innocence and their covenant loyalty. The communal stoning afterward distributes responsibility across the covenant community.
10: 'You shall stone him to death, because he tried to turn you away from the LORD your God who brought you out of Egypt, out of the house of slavery' — the execution is justified by invoking the redemption narrative: the attempted apostate-enticement is a rejection of the YHWH who redeemed Israel from Egypt. The exodus is not merely historical background but the covenant's defining event — to lead Israel away from YHWH is to attempt to undo the redemption itself and return Israel to the condition of slavery.
11: 'And all Israel shall hear and be afraid' — the public deterrence function of executing the apostate-enticer. The execution is to be known throughout Israel (kol-yiśrāʾēl yišmĕʿû wĕyîrāʾûn) so that 'no one ever does such an evil thing among you again.' ANE legal codes regularly cited public fear as the purpose of capital punishment — the visible consequence instructs the community. The fear generated is not personal cowardice but the healthy reverence (yārēʾ) that corresponds to YHWH's own moral weight.
12: 'If you hear in one of your cities that someone is saying' — the third scenario scales from individual (prophet: Deuteronomy 13:1-5) and family unit (close relative: Deuteronomy 13:6-11) to an entire city. The report-and-investigation model assumes the covenant community has an information network: rumors of apostasy in a distant city reach the broader community, triggering a formal inquiry. This reflects the ANE administrative reality of a tribal confederation where misconduct in one area affected the whole covenant body.
13: 'Certain worthless men have gone out from among you' — ʾănāšîm bĕnê-bĕliyaʿal: men of Belial/worthlessness. Bĕlîyaʿal (from bĕlî yāʿal: without profit/worth) is the standard Deuteronomic term for covenant rebels (cf. 1 Samuel 2:12; Deuteronomy 25:17; 2 Kings 2:12). The personification of Belial as a demonic figure develops in Second Temple literature (Dead Sea Scrolls; 2 Corinthians 6:15) and is applied by Paul to the category of covenant-breakers. The 'worthless' men who 'go out from among you' — abandoning the covenant community to lead a city into apostasy — are the urban-scale equivalent of the individual apostate-enticer.
14: 'Then you shall investigate thoroughly and inquire carefully' — the requirement of thorough investigation before execution: dārašt wĕḥāqartā wĕšāʾaltā heṭēb (you shall diligently inquire, carefully investigate, and ask thoroughly). Three different investigation verbs reinforce that the judicial process must be exhaustive before any penalty. This procedural requirement prevents mob violence against an innocent city on false reports. The Deuteronomic legal system consistently requires careful evidentiary procedures before capital verdicts (Deuteronomy 17:4; Deuteronomy 19:18).
15: 'You shall certainly strike down the inhabitants of that city with the sword, devoting it to complete destruction' — the ḥērem (ban/devotion to destruction) applied to a whole Israelite city that has apostatized. The application of the same war-ban used against Canaanite cities (Deuteronomy 7:2; Deuteronomy 20:17) to an Israelite city represents the ultimate internal purging: an apostate Israelite city is equivalent to an enemy Canaanite city and receives the same treatment. The livestock are included — all living things that participated in the apostate city's life are devoted.
16: 'You shall gather all its plunder into the center of its public square and burn the city and all its plunder as a whole offering to the LORD your God' — the apostate city's wealth is burned as a kālîl (whole burnt offering) to YHWH: not distributed as spoil (as in normal conquest) but entirely destroyed. The public square burning requires concentration and visibility — the entire community must witness the ḥērem's execution, confirming it is complete. 'It shall be a permanent ruin; it must never be rebuilt' — the permanent desolation (tel) as a lasting covenant testimony. Several Canaanite destruction sites excavated by archaeologists preserve this physical 'tell' testimony.
17: 'None of the devoted things shall cling to your hand, so that the LORD may turn from his burning anger' — the warning against the Achan-pattern: taking forbidden devoted items as personal spoil. Achan's violation of the Jericho ḥērem (Josh 7) demonstrates the covenant danger of contaminating the community with devoted goods. The ḥērem works only if completely executed; partial obedience — keeping the best items — is treated as a covenant violation that puts the whole community under YHWH's anger.
18: 'When you obey the voice of the LORD your God by keeping all his commandments' — the chapter closes with the covenant compliance formula, as Deuteronomy 12 did (Deuteronomy 13:28). The entire legal code of Deuteronomy 12-26 is framed by this formula: faithful execution of the covenant's laws (including the harsh measures against apostasy) is the condition for YHWH's compassion and the fulfillment of the patriarchal multiplication promise. The severity of the apostasy laws is not punitive excess but the covenantal cost of maintaining the exclusive loyalty that the Shema demands.
